stores.name: モリタ屋 クオリティフードマーケット 本店 (Moritaya Quality Food Market Honten)
stores.gf_summary: The store's stated core selection is organic, additive-free, and pesticide-free products (有機・無添加・無農薬中心), with a macrobiotic (マクロビ) product range — categories that typically include tamari soy sauce, rice-based staples, and whole-grain or unrefined condiments useful to GF shoppers. No dedicated gluten-free corner or labelled GF products are confirmed in any source; treat specific GF items (e.g. rice-flour products, GF pasta) as unconfirmed — ask in-store.
stores.vegan_summary: Direct-sourced organic produce (産直有機青果) is a headline category, and the macrobiotic product range will include plant-based staples. Standard Japanese grocery items like tofu, natto, and soy milk are expected but not individually confirmed; no imported vegan specialty brands are documented.

stores.name: フェリス食養館 北大路大宮店 (Felice Shokuyokan Kitaoji Omiya)
stores.gf_summary: 食養館 is a health-food and supplement specialist (not a general supermarket), stocking additive-free seasonings (無添加調味料), enzyme drinks, organic foods, and natural-food products — categories that regularly include tamari and rice-based items useful to a GF shopper. No confirmed dedicated GF corner or GF-labelled section was found in any source; ask staff or check shelves on arrival.
stores.vegan_summary: The store's focus on 無添加 (additive-free) natural foods and supplements means plant-friendly staples such as additive-free seasonings and health-oriented foods are likely present, but there is no evidence of a dedicated vegan or plant-based grocery range; fresh produce, tofu, or plant-based meat are not part of the confirmed product mix.

stores.name: ナチュラルフーズどんぐり (Natural Foods Donguri)
stores.gf_summary: As a natural/health food shop, Donguri carries soy-based meat substitutes (daizu marugoto meat slices), tempeh, organic and naturally-grown vegetables, and a range of seasonings and imported Italian goods (including pasta — some lines likely GF). Deep Kyoto notes that the store stocks these items but that some meat substitutes elsewhere "are not suitable for the gluten intolerant," so shoppers should check labels; tamari-style soy sauce and rice-based staples are likely present but unconfirmed by name. Ask in-store for specific GF labeling.
stores.vegan_summary: Good base for plant-based self-caterers: soy-based meat analogues (daizu marugoto), tempeh, organic seasonal vegetables, and health-oriented seasonings form the core stock. Imported Italian olive oils and natural condiments round out the offer; no confirmed plant-based dairy or dedicated vegan section reported.

stores.name: おいでやす 森田農園 (Oideyas Morita Noen (Morita Farm Direct Sales))
stores.gf_summary: No dedicated gluten-free section or packaged GF products. The stand sells ~40 types of fresh vegetables (Kyoto traditional varieties such as Kamo eggplant and Shogoin turnip, plus common and Western greens), suguki pickles, and a suguki-based dressing — all naturally gluten-free, but a traveler should check the dressing label for soy sauce/shoyu before purchasing. No tamari, rice-flour products, or imported free-from brands are stocked.
stores.vegan_summary: Excellent for plant-based self-caterers: the entire product range is vegetable-based — fresh seasonal produce including edamame, tomatoes, spinach, komatsuna, mizuna, daikon, and rice in season. The suguki pickles and dressing are also plant-derived. No meat, fish, dairy, or eggs are sold at the stand.

stores.name: イオンモール京都桂川 オーガニックプラザ (Organic Plaza (Aeon Mall Kyoto Katsuragawa))
stores.gf_summary: The store carries "おこめん" — a rice-flour (米粉) noodle product that is explicitly gluten-free — confirmed in a TOKK review of the opening. As an organic specialty chain stocking additive-free (無添加) condiments and fermented foods, it very likely carries tamari-style soy sauce and miso; however, a dedicated GF section or broader certified GF range has not been confirmed from available sources.
stores.vegan_summary: As an organic specialty store (有機野菜、オーガニック食品、無添加調味料), it stocks fresh organic produce, organic processed foods, and additive-free seasonings — a useful base for plant-based self-caterers. Specific lines such as tofu, natto, soy milk, or imported vegan brands have not been confirmed in any published review and should be verified in-store.

stores.name: オーガニックショップ吉田 (Organic Shop Yoshida)
stores.gf_summary: A 30+ year-old macrobiotic-oriented natural food shop stocking Ohsawa Japan (オーサワジャパン) products, which routinely include tamari, whole-grain brown rice (玄米), rice-based products, and additive-free condiments — all useful GF staples. No explicit GF corner or GF labeling is documented in available sources; shoppers should ask staff, but the macrobiotic lineup makes this one of Kyoto's more reliably GF-friendly independents for pantry items.
stores.vegan_summary: The shop's organic and macrobiotic focus strongly suggests organic tofu, soy milk, natto, seaweed, and plant-based condiments (miso, natural soy sauce) are stocked, alongside fresh organic vegetables sourced from the Ohara area. Specific plant-based meat or imported vegan brands are not confirmed in available sources.

stores.name: フレンドフーズ下鴨店 (Friend Foods Shimogamo)
stores.gf_summary: No dedicated gluten-free corner is documented, but Friend Foods' strict "additive-free, authentic production" buying philosophy means it stocks traditionally brewed wooden-barrel soy sauce (naturally GF-compatible), specialty condiments, traceable domestic rice, seasonal produce, and free-range eggs — useful staples for a GF self-caterer. Explicitly labelled GF products or a rice-flour/free-from section are unconfirmed; ask in-store.
stores.vegan_summary: Standard Japanese produce, eggs, and likely tofu/atsuage are stocked as a quality supermarket; the store also carries specialty imported and artisanal condiments that may suit plant-based cooking. No confirmed plant-based meat, soy milk, or dedicated vegan section found in sources — unconfirmed beyond basics.

stores.name: エナジィ・ママ (Energii Mama)
stores.gf_summary: Stocks wholesale natural-food lines オーサワジャパン and ムソー, both of which carry GF-certified tamari (wheat-free soy sauce) and domestic rice flour (米粉); the store's broader range of additive-free, traditionally brewed soy sauces and misos also includes wheat-light options. No dedicated GF corner or GF labelling is confirmed — shoppers should check packaging or ask staff.
stores.vegan_summary: Strong produce base (pesticide-free/organic local Kyoto vegetables, fruits, rice) plus tofu (三之助豆腐, domestic soybean/nigari), soy milk (よつば乳業, 木次乳業), and the オーサワ/ムソー processed-food range which typically includes natto, konjac, and plant-based seasonings; free-range eggs and some seafood/meat are also stocked, so it is not vegan-only, but plant staples are well represented.

stores.name: グリーンマーケットMOA京都店 (Green Market MOA Kyoto)
stores.chain: Green Market MOA (株式会社MOA商事)
stores.gf_summary: Green Market MOA is a natural-farming specialty food shop whose chain-wide range includes rice flour (米粉) products, tamari, tofu, natto, konjac products, and additive-minimised processed foods — all useful for GF shoppers. There is no evidence of a dedicated gluten-free corner or certified-GF labelling section at the Kyoto store specifically; confirm individual items in-store as product mix may vary from the online shop.
stores.vegan_summary: Strong plant-based base: the chain sells MOA natural-farming seasonal produce, tofu, natto, soy milk, and a range of condiments and processed foods minimising additives. Tabelog lists "vegetarian menu available" (ベジタリアンメニューあり), suggesting the Kyoto store stocks enough for self-catering plant-based travelers.

stores.name: ファーマーズ 河原町丸太町店 (Farmers Kawaramachi Marutamachi)
stores.chain: オーガニックプラザ (Organic Plaza)
stores.gf_summary: As an organic/additive-free natural food store and authorized オーサワジャパン retailer, it very likely carries tamari (wheat-free soy sauce), brown-rice-based noodles, and plain organic produce — the staples a GF traveler would seek. No dedicated GF corner is documented, and at least some prepared/processed items (notably certain soy-protein meat substitutes) contain wheat, so label-reading remains essential.
stores.vegan_summary: Strong plant-based selection: fresh organic produce, tofu and pickled vegetables, miso, noodles, rice, vegan butter, vegan chocolate spread, imported vegan snacks and protein bars, kombucha, soy-based meat substitutes, and in-house prepared deli items made with additive-free ingredients. The HappyCow community rates it positively for vegan variety.

stores.name: フレスコ 烏丸店 (Fresco Karasuma)
stores.gf_summary: Fresco is a standard neighborhood super-convenience-store with no dedicated GF or free-from section confirmed. A self-catering GF traveler can rely on naturally safe basics: plain rice, fresh produce, eggs, plain tofu, unprocessed fish and meat, and konjac products (shirataki/konnyaku), which are stocked at virtually all Japanese supermarkets. Tamari (wheat-free soy sauce) may be on the soy-sauce shelf but is not advertised as a feature; imported GF brands and rice-flour products are unconfirmed — treat as ask/unconfirmed.
stores.vegan_summary: Standard Japanese supermarket vegan staples will be available: tofu, atsuage, natto, edamame, fresh produce, rice, and likely soy milk (豆乳). No evidence of plant-based meat, imported vegan specialty lines, or a dedicated plant-based section; the 24-hour format and full food range make it reliable for produce and soy-based proteins.

stores.name: モリタ屋クオリティフードマーケット SUINA室町店 (Moritaya Quality Food Market SUINA Muromachi)
stores.gf_summary: No dedicated GF section, but the store's curated "select-shop" grocery range includes organic produce, macrobiotic こだわり foods (confirmed by multiple sources), konnyaku noodles (naturally GF), and artisan soy sauces via 職人醤油 — their catalogue includes wheat-free tamari, though specific stock at this branch is unconfirmed and should be verified in-store. A self-caterer can reliably source naturally GF staples (fresh meat, organic vegetables, eggs, fish) plus potentially tamari and specialty free-from pantry items.
stores.vegan_summary: Organic and farm-direct vegetables are a confirmed store highlight; tofu is listed among daily staples; one source references macrobiotic product lines (マクロビ商品等のこだわり食品). Plant-based selection beyond produce and tofu is unconfirmed — this is primarily a premium wagyu beef market, so vegan shoppers will find good fresh organic produce and tofu basics but should not expect a wide dedicated plant-based range.

stores.name: フェリス食養館 四条河原町店 (Feliz Shokuyokan Shijo Kawaramachi)
stores.chain: 株式会社フェリス (健康市場食養館)
stores.gf_summary: As a natural/health-food shop (自然食品店) carrying additive-free seasonings (無添加調味料), organic foods, and enzyme beverages, it will typically stock tamari-style soy sauce, rice-based products, and naturally GF health items, though no dedicated GF corner or GF labeling program has been confirmed in available sources — ask in-store to confirm specific items.
stores.vegan_summary: The chain's documented lines (enzyme drinks, barley grass juice, additive-free organic seasonings, natural foods) are largely plant-based; standard Japanese natural-food stores of this type carry soy milk, tofu, natto, and organic produce, but no plant-based meat or imported vegan brands are specifically confirmed for this location.

stores.name: イオンモールKYOTO（マーケットガーデンKOHYO京都店） (AEON Mall KYOTO (Market Garden KOHYO Kyoto))
stores.gf_summary: As an AEON-group supermarket, KOHYO Kyoto stocks TOPVALU's "やさしごはん" allergen-free line (rice-flour bread, rice-flour spaghetti/fettuccine/yakisoba noodles, sprouted brown rice pasta, rice-flour white sauce, rice-flour baking mix — all free of the 8 mandatory allergens including wheat) alongside AEON-brand rice-flour noodles (udon-style, chuka-style, yakisoba-style) and chickpea-brown rice spaghetti. A dedicated free-from corner is standard in larger AEON/KOHYO branches; specific in-store signage for this branch is unconfirmed — contact 075-693-5040 to verify.
stores.vegan_summary: The supermarket carries standard Japanese vegan staples — tofu, natto, atsuage, konnyaku, and a wide produce section including Kyoto vegetables; soy milk and plant-based dairy alternatives are typically stocked in KOHYO/AEON branches. TOPVALU やさしごはん dairy-free chocolate and okara cookies are listed AEON-chain products, though availability at this specific branch is unconfirmed.

stores.name: プレマルシェ・オーガニクス 京都三条 (Premarché Organics Kyoto Sanjo)
stores.chain: プレマルシェ・オーガニクス
stores.gf_summary: Premarché Organics explicitly maintains a "グルテンフリー・小麦不使用" (gluten-free/wheat-free) product category among its ~3,000-item range, alongside organic grains, natural seasonings, and an "オルタナティブフード（代替食品）" (alternative foods) section — making it a genuine free-from destination rather than a general supermarket. Stocked brands include Osawa, Marusima, and Muso, all of which commonly supply tamari, rice products, and GF-labelled foods; specific in-stock SKUs should be confirmed on-site or via their online store (binchoutan.com).
stores.vegan_summary: The store carries its own Prema Shanti natural-food line plus a broad range of organic plant foods (grains, beans, oils, teas) and explicitly lists vegan-friendly options among its health food selection. The adjacent Premarché Gelateria on the same premises offers vegan/non-dairy gelato, and the nearby Premarché Alternative Diner caters to plant-based diners — making the complex a strong base for vegan self-caterers in central Kyoto.

stores.name: 水の子関西 山科店 (Mizunoko Kansai Yamashina)
stores.gf_summary: As a natural/organic food specialist, the store carries handmade additive-free seasonings (almost certainly including tamari or naturally-brewed soy sauce), pesticide-free rice, and additive-free retort and packaged foods — categories that overlap substantially with gluten-free needs. No dedicated GF corner or GF labeling is confirmed from available sources; ask in-store about specific products.
stores.vegan_summary: The store's focus on organic produce, additive-free foods, and natural health products makes it well-suited for plant-based shoppers: expect pesticide-free/organic vegetables, rice-based staples, and likely tofu, natto, and soy milk from small producers, plus organic snacks and supplements. Specific plant-based meat or imported vegan brands are unconfirmed.

stores.name: ライフ四条烏丸店 (Life Shijo Karasuma)
stores.chain: Life (ライフコーポレーション)
stores.gf_summary: This store carries Life's Bio-Ral (ビオラル) private-brand health corner, which includes confirmed gluten-free items sold chain-wide in Kansai: rice-flour cream puffs (国産米粉シュークリーム), rice-flour + soy-flour + quinoa cookies, tamari soy sauce, and imported GF retort soups (AUGA minestrone). Beyond the Bio-Ral corner, the store stocks standard naturally-GF staples — rice, produce, plain meat and fish, eggs, and tofu — making it genuinely useful for a self-catering GF traveler, though it is not a dedicated free-from shop.
stores.vegan_summary: The Bio-Ral section carries organic soy milk (有機豆乳), tofu and soy-processed goods (大豆加工品), and has periodically stocked additive-free vegan retort meals (Good Good Series). The store's standard floor offers tofu, natto, atsuage, and a full produce section, giving plant-based self-caterers a solid everyday shopping base.

stores.name: ファーマーズ（ナチュラルフードスーパーマーケット） (Farmers Natural Food Supermarket)
stores.gf_summary: Carries rice-flour (米粉) snacks, imported naturally-GF grains (quinoa, oatmeal, muesli), organic produce, and additive-free seasonings — useful picks for a GF self-caterer. No dedicated GF section is documented; one source flags the store's wheat-protein meat substitutes as unsuitable for gluten-intolerant shoppers, so label-checking is essential. Tamari availability is unconfirmed.
stores.vegan_summary: Strong plant-based range for a small natural supermarket: tofu and soy products, fresh organic/pesticide-free produce, vegan butter, veggie ramen, plant-protein blocks, and imported grains. The store also stocks meat and fish so it is not vegan-only.

stores.name: ポケットくらぶ (Pocket Club)
stores.gf_summary: As a dedicated natural food shop (40+ years), Pocket Club explicitly stocks gluten-free and vegan-labeled products alongside its additive-free and organic range. Confirmed categories include natural seasonings (notably tamari-style soy sauce from Kamebishi), organic dried goods, and health confectionery; rice-based and free-from staples are expected to be on shelf, though the exact GF SKU list is unconfirmed from online sources alone — ask staff on arrival.
stores.vegan_summary: The store is listed on kyotovegan.jp as a vegetarian/vegan-friendly shop and explicitly carries vegan-labeled products. Its range of organic produce, additive-free seasonings, dried goods, and retort/canned items provides solid plant-based self-catering options; tofu, natto, and soy milk availability is likely but unconfirmed from web sources.

stores.name: 津乃吉 (Tsunokichi)
stores.gf_summary: 津乃吉 is a specialty preserved-foods shop selling chirimen-sansho (baby sardines with sansho pepper), beef shigure-ni, chicken liver preparations, and pickled vegetables — virtually all made with soy sauce and/or fish-based dashi. There is no GF labelling, no free-from corner, and no rice or plain-staple category; a self-catering GF shopper will find nothing useful here.
stores.vegan_summary: The shop's core range is built on fish (chirimen sardines) and meat (beef, chicken liver), with soy sauce used throughout. Pickled vegetables (tsukemono-style) may be the only nominally plant-based items, but soy sauce content and shared production make them unreliable for strict vegans; no plant-based or vegan products are listed or marketed.

stores.name: 成城石井 コトチカ京都店 (Seijo Ishii Kotochika Kyoto)
stores.gf_summary: Seijo Ishii chain-wide carries a solid range of useful GF shelf items: tamari (e.g. Denemon 100% soybean tamari), rice-flour fresh pasta (Kobayashi Seimen, 27-allergen-free), imported organic GF spaghetti (Alcenero corn-rice blend), rice-flour bread and bagels (Tainai), rice-flour biscuits (Nuria, imported from Spain), and imported GF snacks and crackers. However, the Kotochika Kyoto branch is a compact station-format store with no fresh produce and no frozen foods, so fresh or frozen GF staples are absent; the GF selection is limited to packaged shelf goods. There is no dedicated GF corner — items are shelved with regular products, so label-reading is required.
stores.vegan_summary: Chain-wide Seijo Ishii stocks vegan-friendly imported items including organic curry roux without animal ingredients, Tom Yam paste (vegan-certified), organic macrobi cookies, and quinoa-chickpea curries; standard tofu, natto, atsuage, and soy milk are typically carried but the no-fresh-produce format at this branch means fresh tofu and chilled plant-based products are unconfirmed — verify in-store. Imported vegan brands and organic condiments are the reliable vegan draw here.

stores.name: 業務スーパー 四条寺町店 (Gyomu Super Shijo Teramachi)
stores.gf_summary: Gyomu Super chain-wide carries a practical range of naturally GF staples useful for self-caterers: own-brand rice flour (米粉, 1 kg, labeled gluten-free using domestic rice), rice paper (ライスペーパー, ¥75), Vietnamese rice noodles/vermicelli (ライスヌードル, made from rice flour and tapioca), and Korean-style dried seaweed. The imported-goods section typically stocks rice noodles, rice paper, and various Asian GF staples; tamari and dedicated GF-labeled pasta are not confirmed at this specific branch — ask in-store.
stores.vegan_summary: Good plant-based options chain-wide include tofu, natto, and atsuage (standard Japanese stock), soy-meat bolognese sauce (ソイボロネーゼ), plant-based soup cubes, soy milk, frozen fruits, oats, hummus, tahini, canned chickpeas, and coconut milk — making it one of the better budget supermarkets in Kyoto for self-catering vegans, especially for imported pantry goods.

stores.name: ゼロウェイスト京都 (Zero Waste Kyoto)
stores.gf_summary: The attached cafe explicitly labels its plant-based quiche as gluten-free (ヴィーガン・グルテンフリー対応), and the brown-rice-centred meal plates are naturally GF. The retail floor sells bulk rice, nuts, legumes (soybeans), extra-virgin olive oil, fig and balsamic vinegars, and tofu by weight — all naturally gluten-free — though the shop also stocks whole-grain wheat flour, so a GF-dedicated section is not confirmed; shoppers should verify labels or ask staff.
stores.vegan_summary: Strong plant-based orientation throughout: bulk tofu (bring your own container), natto subscription service (reusable bento box), large soybeans, sesame paste, miso, organic vegetables, and a cafe whose signature "My Gentle Feast" plate centres brown rice and seasonal vegetable side dishes. The plant-based quiche is explicitly vegan.

stores.name: Biople 京都髙島屋S.C.店 (Biople Kyoto Takashimaya S.C. (T8))
stores.gf_summary: Biople is primarily an organic cosmetics and wellness boutique, but the chain also stocks a curated selection of natural/organic food — including snacks, energy bars, seeds, condiments, and organic beverages — many of which are inherently gluten-free. The brand's web store has an explicit GF product category, suggesting physical stores stock at least some GF-labelled items; however, no dedicated GF corner has been confirmed and one visitor described the food section as small and limited. Ask in-store for GF-specific products.
stores.vegan_summary: HappyCow lists the store as carrying vegan-friendly cookies, snacks, energy bars, seeds, and condiments alongside cosmetics. One vegan reviewer found the plant-based food selection disappointingly small for protein options, so it suits grab-and-go snacking better than full self-catering provisioning.

stores.name: オーガニックプラザ修学院店 (Organic Plaza Shugakuin)
stores.gf_summary: Organic Plaza is an organic specialty chain that, across its Kyoto locations, stocks organic brown rice noodles (gluten-free), Osawa-brand organic tamari (wheat-free soy sauce), additive-free seasonings, and imported free-from brands such as Alishan and Fiordifrutta. No dedicated GF corner or GF labeling system is confirmed for the Shugakuin store specifically — treat specific GF-labeled stock as likely but unconfirmed until in-store; ask staff.
stores.vegan_summary: Chain locations carry vegan staples including fresh organic vegetables, plant-based bouillon (Osawa vegetable broth), organic brown rice noodles, raw nuts and dried fruits (Alishan), vegan chocolate (Nocciolata), and additive-free breads; tofu and soy milk are standard at Japanese organic stores of this type but not explicitly confirmed for this branch.

stores.name: 業務スーパー 北白川店 (Gyōmu Super Kitashirakawa)
stores.gf_summary: Gyōmu Super carries several useful GF items chain-wide (stocked in individual sections, not a dedicated GF corner): own-brand rice flour (米粉 1 kg bag), rice noodles/ライスヌードル (rice + tapioca starch, good for pho), rice paper (ライスペーパー), Korean dried seaweed, imported fruit/date snack bars, plain tomato-basil pizza sauce, pickled vegetables, and konjac products. Tamari is not confirmed in stock; shoppers should check the soy-sauce aisle. No labelled GF section — items must be sought by ingredient.
stores.vegan_summary: Plant-based options are a relative strength: a frozen soy bolognese (ソイボロネーゼ, 680 g) is stocked chain-wide, alongside tofu, natto, atsuage, large bags of rice and dried legumes, an expansive produce section, and imported Southeast-Asian sauces (gapao paste, etc.) that are often vegan. Soy milk availability is typical for the chain but confirm in-store.

stores.name: 出町輸入食品 (Demachi Yunyuu Shokuhin (Demachi Imported Foods))
stores.gf_summary: This shop is a specialty import store focused almost entirely on self-roasted coffee beans and imported confectionery (cookies, biscuits, chocolate, wafers, candy, crackers, jam, cake). No gluten-free section, free-from labeling, tamari, rice-flour products, GF pasta, or imported free-from brands are mentioned anywhere across the official site, review platforms, or press coverage — ask/unconfirmed if any incidental GF items exist among the imported snacks. Not useful for a self-catering GF traveler.
stores.vegan_summary: No plant-based grocery staples (tofu, natto, soy milk, plant-based meat, produce) are stocked; the store sells imported sweets and coffee, not grocery lines. Incidentally vegan items may exist among some imported cookies, but nothing is confirmed or marketed as vegan.

stores.name: ローズマルシェ グローサリー (Rose Marche Grocery)
stores.gf_summary: As a Meidi-ya-operated premium imported-food grocer, this shop is known to carry some GF-useful items: industry sources note Meidi-ya branches "often carry GF items" — expect tamari, imported olive oils, rice-based crackers or snacks, and potentially GF pasta from European brands (Garofalo also produces a GF rice-pasta line). No dedicated free-from corner is confirmed for this specific location; ask staff or check shelf labels.
stores.vegan_summary: The imported-food grocery format means a self-caterer will find extra-virgin olive oil, canned tomatoes, imported legumes, pasta, jams, vinegar, and wine — all naturally vegan staples. No plant-based meat, soy milk, or dedicated vegan brand range has been documented for this location.

stores.name: ライフ 宝ヶ池店 (Life Takaraike-ten)
stores.gf_summary: Life's BIO-RAL private label — stocked at most branches including Kansai stores — includes gluten-free rice-flour cookies (domestic rice flour + quinoa), organic silken tofu (defoamer-free), organic miso, organic tsuyu, and multi-grain brown rice packs; these are naturally or explicitly GF. A BIO-RAL corner is standard in most Life stores. No dedicated GF specialist section and no confirmed tamari or labelled GF soy sauce at this branch specifically — shoppers should check the natural-foods corner on arrival.
stores.vegan_summary: Good baseline for plant-based self-catering: BIO-RAL organic silken tofu, standard natto and atsuage (fresh section), produce, plain rice and konjac staples, and BIO-RAL organic miso and sesame are all vegan. BIO-RAL curry roux made without animal ingredients has been sold at some Kinki-area branches; soy milk availability is typical for this chain size but unconfirmed for this specific outlet.

stores.name: フレスコ 北野白梅町店 (Fresco Kitano Hakubaicho)
stores.chain: Fresco (フレスコ / スーパーフレスコ)
stores.gf_summary: Fresco is a mainstream 24-hour regional chain with no documented GF section or free-from specialty range; a self-catering GF shopper will find naturally gluten-free staples — plain rice, fresh produce, eggs, plain meat and fish, tofu, natto, konjac, and mochi-based wagashi (the chain's own okara and hijiki obanzai sides are also naturally GF). Tamari (wheat-free soy sauce) is unconfirmed at this store; standard shoyu is the norm at mainstream chains, so shoppers should bring their own or verify in-store.
stores.vegan_summary: Standard Japanese supermarket plant-based staples are well covered: multiple tofu grades (silken, firm, atsuage), natto, konjac products, and soy milk are all typical Fresco stock, and the chain's own obanzai line includes okara no taitan and hijiki no taitan — traditional Kyoto plant-based side dishes. No evidence of dedicated plant-based meat or imported vegan specialty brands at this location.

stores.name: プレマルシェ・オーガニクス 黒門ヤード (Premarché Organics Kuromon Yard)
stores.chain: プレマルシェ・オーガニクス（プレマ株式会社）
stores.gf_summary: The store and its online arm (binchoutan.com) carry a dedicated グルテンフリー・小麦不使用 (gluten-free/wheat-free) category and an オルタナティブフード section. Confirmed items include the own-brand Prema Shanti 米粉 (rice flour, 1 kg), Felicia gluten-free pasta (corn/rice/quinoa/buckwheat, Celiac Association certified), Kobayashi GF kale pasta, and a vegan-and-GF dorayaki; frozen allergen-free items (top-7 free) are also stocked. With ~3,000 products in-store from a 10,000-item natural-foods catalogue, this is the strongest dedicated GF destination in central Kyoto.
stores.vegan_summary: Listed as a vegan-friendly store in Kansai natural-food guides; the range covers whole grains, organic produce, miso, natural seasonings, plant-based alternative foods, and herbal teas. The adjacent Premarché Gelateria offers vegan and non-dairy gelato, and the same building complex houses Premarché Alternative Diner (plant-based dining, currently temporarily closed).

stores.name: the kind（ザ カインド） (the kind)
stores.gf_summary: Individual items carry gluten-free labels where applicable; concretely GF-safe picks in the bulk bins include chickpeas, red and green lentils, quinoa, chia and pumpkin seeds, coconut chips, dried fruits, nuts, and fresh-ground nut butters. A "vegetable crisps" snack is explicitly labeled vegan and gluten-free; oatmeal is stocked but standard Japanese oats are not certified GF, so celiacs should skip those. No dedicated GF corner — GF labeling is per-item.
stores.vegan_summary: Strong plant-based lineup throughout: soy meat (大豆ミート), soy milk, organic oat milk, vegan chocolate (70–72% cacao), curry spice blends, and the full dried-fruit/nut/legume/seed range is inherently vegan. The attached café is plant-based.

stores.name: 豊受自然農の健康食生活 京都店 (Toyouke Shizenno no Kenko Shokukatsu Kyoto-ten)
stores.gf_summary: The shop stocks 200+ organic/natural health items but has no dedicated gluten-free section. Naturally GF staples are available — own-farm rice (玄米/白米), deep-sea salt, nigari, additive-free pickled plums (梅干し), and herbal teas — but the shop also sells wheat flour and whole-wheat flour, and their signature soy sauce is a wheat-bearing "herb soy sauce" (生醤油), not tamari. No rice-flour (米粉) products, GF pasta, or free-from labeling was confirmed.
stores.vegan_summary: Strong plant-based orientation throughout: the restaurant serves thick-fried tofu (厚揚げ) sets, soy-milk potages, and sugar-free vegetable desserts; the shop carries their own white miso, salts, herbal teas, and organic produce. No animal-free certification is stated, but the largely vegetable-and-grain philosophy makes this a practical stop for vegan shoppers seeking additive-free pantry staples.

stores.name: イオンスタイル京都五条 (AEON Style Kyoto Gojo)
stores.gf_summary: As a full-size AEON Style supermarket, this store carries AEON's own TopValu やさしごはん allergen-free (8-allergen including wheat-free) line, which includes rice-flour pasta (spaghetti, fettuccine, yakisoba styles), rice-flour soft bread, baking mixes, rice-stick snacks, cookies, and allergen-free white sauce and curry. AEON also stocks rice-flour-based frozen items (takoyaki, gyoza wrappers), Nippon Ham rice-bread, and branded GF ramen (TSURUMEN soy sauce and plant-based tonkotsu); no dedicated in-store GF corner is documented for this specific branch, so stock depth vs. the chain catalogue should be treated as unconfirmed and product availability verified in-store.
stores.vegan_summary: AEON Style stores carry a wide tofu/natto/atsuage selection plus soy milk (including AEON and third-party brands); the TopValu やさしごはん range includes dairy-free chocolate and plant-based options, and AEON stocks imported vegan pantry staples and ample fresh produce, konjac products, and rice noodles. The store's sizeable produce and prepared-foods sections (noted for abundant sōzai/bento) provide good naturally plant-based options, though prepared foods may contain hidden animal ingredients.

stores.name: 健らいと (Kenraito)
stores.gf_summary: 健らいと is a natural/additive-free food shop that stocks flour products (粉類) and, per its own website product pages (now offline), rice noodles made with 北海道産ゆきひかり rice flour and tapioca starch — a naturally gluten-free item. As a no-additive natural food specialist it very likely carries tamari-style soy sauce and rice-based staples, but no dedicated GF corner or GF labeling has been confirmed; shoppers should ask in store.
stores.vegan_summary: The store's no-additive, natural-food orientation suggests it stocks plant-based staples such as additive-free seasonings, organic confections, and health foods; specific vegan lines (tofu, natto, plant-based milk) are unconfirmed from available sources — ask in store.

stores.name: 坂ノ途中soil (Sakanotochu Soil)
stores.gf_summary: No dedicated GF corner or labeling, but the shop's core stock is GF-friendly: abundant seasonal vegetables, rice, and a curated condiment range that includes pure rice miso (米味噌), rice koji (米糀), pure rice vinegar (純米酢), coconut oil, sesame oil, and olive oil. The soy sauces stocked are standard wheat-containing shoyu — no tamari was confirmed in the in-store range — so GF shoppers should check labels carefully on any soy-based condiments.
stores.vegan_summary: Vegan sweets (ヴィーガンスイーツ) are an explicitly stocked category, selected by staff. The shop's wide seasonal vegetable selection and plant-based pantry staples (rice, koji, vinegars, plant oils, seaweed/nori, barley tea, Laotian coffee) make it a strong stop for plant-based self-caterers; no animal products are central to the offering.

stores.name: HELP 洛北阪急スクエア店 (HELP Rakuhoku Hankyu Square)
stores.gf_summary: HELP is a 40-year-old specialist natural and organic food retailer explicitly focused on minimising chemical fertilisers, pesticides, and synthetic additives — the stock profile strongly overlaps with GF-friendly shopping (additive-free seasonings, organic rice, contract-farm vegetables, non-GMO private-label "素のまま" condiments including apple-vinegar mayonnaise and rice). No dedicated gluten-free signage or labelled GF corner was confirmed in available sources; ask in-store for tamari and rice-flour products, which are standard at this type of natural food shop.
stores.vegan_summary: The store's organic and additive-free focus means a solid base of plant-based staples: organically grown vegetables and fruit from contract farmers, rice, additive-free seasonings, and their own non-GMO private-label line; tofu, natto, and soy milk are expected given the natural-foods positioning, though not individually named in retrieved pages — confirm on a visit.

stores.name: 成城石井 四条烏丸店 (Seijo Ishii Shijo Karasuma)
stores.gf_summary: Seijo Ishii chain is widely regarded as one of Japan's best supermarkets for GF shoppers: this store carries gluten-free pasta (Alcenero organic corn/rice-flour spaghetti, various shapes), Tainai rice-flour bread, multiple GF pancake mixes, imported GF snacks, and a gluten-free tamari-style soy sauce (Sanjirushi brand). No dedicated GF corner — items are scattered across departments — and crucially this station-concourse location does NOT stock fresh produce or frozen foods, limiting full self-catering use; best for pantry staples and specialty dry/packaged GF items.
stores.vegan_summary: Carries BEYOND TOFU block and natural variants, NOHEA vegan mayo, Marukome Daizu Lab soy meat (diced), almond milk yogurt, organic quinoa curry (no animal ingredients), imported dark chocolate, dried fruits, and various plant-based condiments; however no fresh tofu, natto, or atsuage because this station-basement format does not carry fresh or frozen goods.

stores.name: ナチュラルプラス (Natural Plus)
stores.gf_summary: As a dedicated macrobiotic and natural food shop, ナチュラルプラス carries additive-free foods and traditionally-made seasonings (including the tamari-style, wheat-reduced soy sauces typical of macrobiotic ranges), organic rice, and organic confections — all categories that overlap heavily with GF-safe staples. No explicitly labelled GF corner is confirmed from public sources; ask in-store about specific GF items such as rice flour products (米粉) and GF-certified seasonings.
stores.vegan_summary: The store's macrobiotic focus (plant-centred whole-foods philosophy) means it very likely stocks soy-based staples (tofu, natto, soy milk), fermented condiments, organic whole grains, and additive-free plant foods; no specific plant-based meat or imported vegan brands are confirmed, so check in-store for current range.

stores.name: マルシェノグチ (Marche Noguchi)
stores.gf_summary: No dedicated gluten-free section or free-from products were found in any source. The shop stocks 60+ varieties of seasonal organic and reduced-pesticide vegetables, fruit, rice (お米), and some processed foods — all naturally GF staples for self-caterers, but ask in-store about specific processed/加工食品 lines as product details are unconfirmed.
stores.vegan_summary: Excellent for plant-based self-caterers: the entire core stock is produce — organic and direct-from-farm seasonal vegetables and fruit, plus rice. Fresh vegetable smoothies (¥350) are also available. No plant-based meat or specialty vegan packaged goods are confirmed, but the produce selection alone is strong.

stores.name: グルメシティ上桂店 (Gourmet City Kamikatsura)
stores.chain: Gourmet City (ダイエー / AEON グループ)
stores.gf_summary: No dedicated gluten-free section or free-from aisle is documented at this Daiei/Gourmet City store. A self-catering GF traveler can rely on naturally GF staples: plain rice, produce, eggs, plain fish and meat, tofu, atsuage, natto, konnyaku, harusame (glass noodles), and possibly rice flour products common in standard Japanese supermarkets. Tamari (GF soy sauce) availability is unconfirmed — ask in-store.
stores.vegan_summary: As a full-range neighborhood supermarket it reliably stocks tofu (silken and firm), atsuage, natto, and soy milk; fresh produce is abundant and competitively priced per reviews. Plant-based meat alternatives and imported vegan specialty brands are not confirmed and unlikely given the standard Daiei format.

stores.name: 田辺自然食品 (Tanabe Shizen Shokuhin)
stores.gf_summary: As an independent 自然食品店 (natural food shop) specialising in 無添加 (additive-free) seasonings and organic goods, it very likely stocks tamari, additive-free miso, rice-based products, and similar inherently GF staples typical of Japanese natural food stores — but no dedicated GF section or labelled GF range is documented in any online source. Shoppers should confirm specific items on arrival; the 無添加 seasoning focus is the most reliable GF entry point.
stores.vegan_summary: Natural food shops of this type routinely carry tofu, natto, atsuage, soy milk, and plant-based condiments (additive-free miso, tamari, sesame oil), but no explicit vegan product line or plant-based meat selection is confirmed for this specific store. The organic and additive-free focus makes it a plausible source of vegan pantry staples; unconfirmed beyond directory description.

stores.name: 斗々屋 京都本店 (Totoya Kyoto Honten)
stores.gf_summary: Totoya sells bulk 米粉 (rice flour), plain rice, tofu, and natto — all naturally GF — as part of its ~700-item zero-waste bulk range; rice flour is explicitly listed as a staple. No dedicated GF section or GF labeling is confirmed, and no tamari was specifically documented, but the organic/artisan bulk condiment selection makes it worth checking in-store. A self-catering GF traveler can reliably source rice, rice flour, tofu, natto, fresh produce, miso, sesame, and dried kelp here.
stores.vegan_summary: Strongly vegan-friendly: the deli counter offers clearly labelled vegan dishes (Buddha bowls, vegan banh mi, tsukemono, soy-meat with black vinegar sauce, vegan cookies/fudge), and bulk staples include tofu, natto, fresh produce, grains, miso, and sesame. HappyCow lists it as a vegan venue with daily-changing vegan specials.

stores.name: グルメシティ北山店 (Gourmet City Kitayama)
stores.gf_summary: As an AEON-group Daiei store, Gourmet City Kitayama stocks TopValu private-brand products including the "やさしごはん" (Yasashigohan) allergen-free line — wheat-free rice noodles, rice flour mixes (ライスフラワー), and allergen-free bread/sweets made without the 8 mandatory allergens — alongside naturally GF staples such as rice, fresh produce, plain meat and fish, eggs, tofu, natto, and konjac. There is no dedicated GF corner or specialist free-from section; availability of the full Yasashigohan range at this specific branch is unconfirmed and should be checked in-store.
stores.vegan_summary: Standard AEON-chain stocking is expected to include tofu, natto, atsuage, konjac products, soy milk, and a wide fresh-produce section; the TopValu Vegetive plant-based line (soy-based cheese alternatives, plant-based puddings) is carried at AEON-group stores but individual branch selection varies. No dedicated vegan section has been reported for this store.

stores.name: アスカ有機農園 (Asuka Yuki Noen)
stores.chain: Independent - Organic Farm Market & Health Foods
stores.gf_summary: The store's own about page explicitly lists グルテンフリー (gluten-free) alongside vegan and macrobiotic as dietary frameworks it caters to, carrying 1,000+ items including additive-free condiments, organic ancient grains (black rice, red rice), pesticide-free white and brown rice, and macrobiotic foods — all naturally GF. No dedicated GF-labelled section or free-from specialty corner is confirmed; tamari and rice-flour products are plausible given the macrobiotic/vegetarian category in the online shop but should be verified in store.
stores.vegan_summary: The about page explicitly names ヴィーガン (vegan) and ベジタリアン (vegetarian) as customer dietary values the store supports, and the online shop lists vegetarian/plant-based and macrobiotic categories alongside organic tofu, additive-free seasonings, and seasonal Kyoto vegetable sets. Plant-based staples (tofu, organic produce, fermented foods like umeboshi and amazake) are well-represented; imported vegan specialty products are unconfirmed.

stores.name: 食彩市場 (Shokusai Ichiba)
stores.gf_summary: No GF section or free-from focus confirmed. The shop stocks fresh seasonal vegetables (including pesticide-free/reduced-pesticide produce from Kyoto and Shiga farms), plain Koshihikari and Kinuhikari rice, and some additive-free condiments and dried goods — all naturally gluten-free basics, but no tamari, rice-flour products, or imported free-from brands were mentioned in any source; ask in-store about condiment ingredients.
stores.vegan_summary: All produce is plant-based; the shop emphasizes seasonal vegetables from small contract farms, plus rice and tea. Some additive-free condiments and dried goods are stocked, though specific vegan suitability (e.g. dashi-free) is unconfirmed — worth asking, as the shop has a health-conscious, minimal-additive ethos.

stores.name: ライフ 伏見深草店 (Life Fushimi Fukakusa Ten)
stores.gf_summary: Life stores carry a BIO-RAL natural-foods corner (confirmed by a reviewer at this branch) stocking GF-labeled rice-flour cookies, organic tamari rice crackers (有機玄米せんべい たまり醤油味), organic silken tofu, organic miso, and — at Kansai stores — GF rice-flour cream puffs; no dedicated GF aisle or specialist focus. Note: the in-store Komugi no Sato wheat bakery is an active cross-contamination risk for celiac shoppers.
stores.vegan_summary: Good baseline for self-caterers: organic silken tofu (有機大豆きぬ豆腐, BIO-RAL), natto, fresh produce including Kyoto vegetables (京野菜), ready-to-heat multigrain brown rice packs, and standard Japanese supermarket staples (atsuage, konjac, soy milk likely stocked); no confirmed dedicated plant-based meat or imported vegan specialty section.

stores.name: やおや ONE DROP (Yaoya One Drop)
stores.gf_summary: ONE DROP is primarily a fresh-produce shop sourcing directly from low-pesticide/organic-leaning farmers; fresh vegetables, free-range eggs, and a small selection of artisan packaged goods (dressings, dashi, inari, confections) are stocked, all naturally or likely GF. No confirmed GF corner, rice-flour (米粉) products, tamari, or dedicated free-from packaged lines were found in any source — ask in-store if unsure.
stores.vegan_summary: Excellent for plant-based self-caterers: the core stock is locally sourced, seasonal vegetables and fruits, ideal for self-catering. Ready-made side dishes (惣菜) are also sold but their vegan status is unconfirmed; eggs are stocked (not vegan). No tofu, soy milk, or plant-based meat lines were mentioned in available sources.

stores.name: ナチュラルフーズ・ドングリ (Natural Foods DONGURI)
stores.gf_summary: This macrobiotic-focused natural foods shop carries additive-free seasonings (including tamari/GF soy sauce typical of macrobiotic ranges), organic produce, imported Italian goods (olive oil, pasta — likely including GF varieties via ORCIO importer), and retort foods. No dedicated gluten-free corner is documented in sources, but the macrobiotic and free-from orientation means useful GF staples are likely on the shelves; confirm specific GF pasta or rice-flour items in-store.
stores.vegan_summary: HappyCow reviewers specifically praise the vegan selection: vegan instant ramen (clearly labeled), soy meat, tinned beans, coconut oil, agave and maple syrup, and a broad range of vegan-labeled packaged products. Staff can help identify vegan items despite limited English, and the macrobiotic ethos makes plant-based the default orientation throughout the store.

stores.name: Organic Vege Annex（オーガニック ベジ アネックス） (Organic Vege Annex)
stores.chain: Independent (original Shimogamo shop est. 2016; Annex branch opened May 2024)
stores.gf_summary: No dedicated gluten-free section or GF-labelled products confirmed. The shop stocks organic vegetables, some additive-free condiments for retail, and VEGE DELI prepared packs — but their own prepared foods use standard wheat-based soy sauce (not tamari), and no rice-flour products, GF pasta, or free-from imports were identified. Naturally GF staples (fresh produce, plain vegetables) are available, but a self-catering GF traveler should not expect a curated free-from range.
stores.vegan_summary: The shop is explicitly listed as "VEGAN対応可能" (vegan-compatible) on the Hachidory ethical/vegan directory. Its VEGE DELI prepared packs centre entirely on vegetables with additive-free seasonings; the core retail offer of pesticide-free seasonal produce and organic condiments is almost entirely plant-based. No animal products are a focus of the business.

stores.name: mumokuteki goods&wears 京都店 (mumokuteki goods&wears Kyoto)
stores.gf_summary: mumokuteki operates a dedicated グルテンフリー (gluten-free) product category online with 140+ items, a subset of which is stocked in the Kyoto store. Confirmed GF retail items include rice-flour (米粉) brownies by Hototogisu, People Tree organic chocolate, crispy mochi bites, nori-wrapped rice crackers, dried umeboshi, and artisan coffees/teas — all naturally or certified GF. The store's natural-goods ethos means GF labeling is applied throughout the food section, making it straightforward for a shopper to identify safe products.
stores.vegan_summary: The store carries an explicit vegan (ヴィーガン) product category: vegan chocolates, cookies, condiments/seasonings, plant-based snacks, and specialty drinks (KIMINO sparkling juices, fruit teas). Vegan items are clearly labeled, and the connected 2F mumokuteki cafe KYOTO is a fully plant-based restaurant, reinforcing the brand's deep vegan orientation. A self-catering traveler can pick up vegan packaged snacks, sweets, and condiments with confidence.

stores.name: 京やさい佐伯 (Kyo-yasai Saeki)
stores.gf_summary: This is a farm-direct vegetable stand selling only fresh produce — no packaged goods, condiments, or specialty GF products. All items are naturally gluten-free (seasonal vegetables: daikon, kabocha, Kujo negi, eggplant, cucumbers, leafy greens, etc.), but a shopper cannot source tamari, rice-flour products, GF pasta, or any processed staples here.
stores.vegan_summary: Excellent for plant-based self-caterers: roughly 70 varieties of pesticide-free organic vegetables sold year-round, including heritage Kyoto vegetables (Shogoin daikon, Fushimi pepper, Shikagadani squash). No animal products sold, so the entire stock is vegan. No tofu, natto, or soy milk — produce only.

stores.name: デイリーカナートイズミヤ 伏見店 (Daily Qanat Izumiya Fushimi-ten)
stores.chain: イズミヤ (Izumiya / Kansai Food Market)
stores.gf_summary: The store has a dedicated "健康＆美容ストリート" (Health & Beauty Street) aisle that stocks allergen-free (アレルゲンフリー) and gluten-free (グルテンフリー) labelled products alongside reduced-salt and low-sugar items; the deli/daily-foods section also carries gluten-free lines. Specific brand names are unconfirmed from available sources — shoppers should check aisle signage on arrival, but a real GF-labelled section exists.
stores.vegan_summary: As a full-size supermarket (2,051 m²), the store stocks standard Japanese plant-based staples: tofu, natto, atsuage, soy milk, and a wide produce section including Kyoto vegetables; a dried fruits and nuts corner is also noted. No dedicated vegan corner or plant-based meat section is confirmed, but mainstream soy-milk and tofu variety is expected to be solid.

stores.name: オーガニックプラザ イオンモール北大路店 (Organic Plaza AEON Mall Kitaoji)
stores.chain: Organic Plaza (HealthLife Co. / Kansai organic specialty chain; tenant in Aeon Mall, not an Aeon-owned brand)
stores.gf_summary: Organic Plaza explicitly stocks gluten-free products as a category across all its branches. Confirmed GF items include 玄米ヌードル (brown rice noodles, GF), 米粉100%餃子の皮 (rice-flour gyoza wrappers), additive-free dashi packs, and chemical-seasoning-free bouillon/consommé. The chain's mission is organic/additive-free specialty retail, making it a reliable stop for GF shoppers seeking labelled free-from alternatives rather than just naturally-GF staples.
stores.vegan_summary: Strong vegan offering: Osawa Japan vegetable bouillon is explicitly labeled for vegans; Beppinpan 100% plant-based bread (amasake-sweetened, no preservatives); Nocciolata vegan hazelnut spread; Alisan raw nuts and dried fruits; organic local produce; and sugar-free organic fruit jams. Additive-free and plant-based items are a core product focus of the chain.

stores.name: 晴れやかファーム うまれたてやさい直売所 (Hareyaka Farm Umaretate Yasai Chokubaijo)
stores.gf_summary: This is a farm-direct produce stand selling JAS-certified organic/pesticide-free fresh vegetables and plain rice from Higashi-Omi, Shiga — all naturally gluten-free, but no dedicated GF section, no tamari, rice flour products, GF pasta, or imported free-from brands are mentioned anywhere. Side items include dried vegetables, vegetable tea, and occasional carrot bread (gluten content unconfirmed).
stores.vegan_summary: Excellent for plant-based self-catering: the entire inventory is produce-focused (seasonal fresh vegetables, certified organic rice, dried veg, vegetable tea), making it a strong stop for stocking up on whole vegetables and rice. Carrot bread appears on Wednesdays and Thursdays but vegan ingredient status is unconfirmed.

stores.name: パントリー京都八百一本館店 (Pantry Kyoto Yaohichi Honkan)
stores.gf_summary: Pantry & Lucky is a Kansai natural/additive-minimising supermarket chain with a confirmed グルテンフリー (gluten-free) product category online and in-store. Verified GF items include organic corn-and-rice GF spaghetti and penne (Alchenero brand), and several Shinshu miso varieties labelled gluten-free; naturally GF staples (produce, eggs, plain meat and fish) are also stocked. No dedicated in-store GF section or tamari/GF soy sauce is confirmed, so the selection is useful but modest rather than specialist.
stores.vegan_summary: Carries organic-certified natto (有機認定大豆使用小粒納豆), sesame tofu (有機煎り胡麻ごまとうふ), a range of miso, and fresh produce. Nakahora Farm dairy is stocked in-store (non-vegan but noted). Plant-based meat and soy milk are not specifically confirmed, though the chain's additive-free natural-food positioning makes standard tofu/natto/produce reliable finds for plant-based self-caterers.

stores.name: カルディコーヒーファーム 京都ポルタ店 (KALDI Coffee Farm Kyoto Porta)
stores.gf_summary: KALDI chain-wide stocks a dedicated range of GF-labelled products that are regularly available in-store: domestic rice-flour pancake mix (国産米粉のパンケーキミックス グルテンフリー), home-bakery GF rice flour, and up to five GF pasta varieties including brown-rice fettuccine, germinated brown-rice macaroni/fusilli, and imported corn-rice spaghetti (Si, Alce Nero organic). GF crackers/crispbread and coconut milk, rice noodles/pho, canned beans, and 100% buckwheat soba are also typically stocked; exact inventory varies by visit, so shoppers should browse the baking and import aisles.
stores.vegan_summary: KALDI carries a broad imported-food assortment useful for plant-based self-catering: chickpea keema curry (大豆ミートとひよこ豆のキーマカレー), chana masala, okara cookies, dried fruit (dates, mango, banana), coconut milk, rice wraps, canned beans, salsas, and various nut/seed snacks — many confirmed vegan on the KALDI online store. No fresh tofu or produce; this is a specialty import grocer, not a full supermarket.

stores.name: HELP 一乗寺店 (HELP Ichijoji)
stores.gf_summary: HELP is a 40-year-old organic/natural foods specialist (自然食品・無添加) whose stock leans GF-friendly by nature: customer photos on Tabelog confirm rice-flour bread (米粉パン) among the baked goods, and the store's focus on additive-free, pesticide-reduced foods means the dry-goods aisle typically carries tamari, naturally-GF condiments, and organic rice. A dedicated labelled GF section is unconfirmed — shoppers should browse the condiment and bakery areas and check labels on arrival.
stores.vegan_summary: As a natural-foods shop the store stocks ample organic produce from contract farmers; organic-leaning Japanese independents of this type routinely carry tofu, natto, atsuage, soy milk, and additive-free miso/tamari. The private-label "素のまま" range covers rice and condiments. Dedicated plant-based meat or imported vegan brands are unconfirmed but plausible given the specialty focus.

stores.name: カルディコーヒーファーム ゼスト御池店 (Kaldi Coffee Farm Zest Oike)
stores.gf_summary: KALDI chain-wide stocks a reliable range of GF staples: corn-and-rice-flour GF spaghetti (Si brand, ~¥500), brown-rice fettuccine, rice flour pancake mix, GF crispbread (Sigdal), GF granola (Nature's Path), corn-chip snacks, and 100% buckwheat soba — confirmed product lines across Japanese GF-focused reviews. No dedicated in-store GF section or specialist labeling is reported; stock rotates and varies by branch, so specific items should be verified on arrival.
stores.vegan_summary: KALDI carries a solid spread of plant-based products: canned chana masala curry, soy-meat pasta sauces (Hokkaido daizu-meat tomato and keema curry pouches), okara cookies, coconut chips, dried fruit, and various Southeast Asian staples (coconut milk, curry pastes, canned beans). Belgian vegan oat-raisin/chocolate cookies (Generous brand) and imported hot sauces, salsas, and corn chips round out the self-catering pantry well.

stores.name: イオン京都洛南ショッピングセンター (Aeon Kyoto Rakunan Shopping Center)
stores.gf_summary: This large-format Aeon supermarket (7:00–23:00 food section) carries Aeon's own Top Valu private-label gluten-free range: rice-flour bread (Nihon Ham and Tainai brands), rice-flour udon/ramen/yakisoba noodles, Top Valu chickpea and brown rice spaghetti, rice-flour panko and gyoza wrappers, and GF snacks such as rice-flour biscuits and brown rice crackers. Tamari and naturally GF staples (plain rice, produce, tofu, fish/meat) are standard stock; a dedicated free-from shelf is unconfirmed for this specific branch but typical of larger Aeon stores.
stores.vegan_summary: Aeon's Top Valu "Vegetive" plant-based line (soy-based hamburgers, karaage-style pieces, ham-katsu alternatives) is sold chain-wide at larger stores; this full-format location is a good candidate to stock it. Standard vegan staples are well-covered: fresh tofu, natto, atsuage, soy milk, a wide produce section, and Top Valu GreenEye Organic processed goods — making it a solid self-catering base for plant-based travelers.

stores.name: カルディコーヒーファーム イオンモールKYOTO店 (KALDI Coffee Farm Aeon Mall KYOTO)
stores.gf_summary: KALDI chain stores carry a reliable selection of imported GF items: GF pasta (notably brown rice fettuccine), rice-flour pancake mixes, GF crackers and crispbread with clear English/bilingual labeling, and 100% buckwheat soba (十割そば). Southeast Asian staples (coconut milk, rice wraps, jasmine rice) and Mexican pantry items (canned beans, corn chips, salsa) add further GF options, though individual labels must still be checked as not all items in those sections are GF.
stores.vegan_summary: The imported-food focus yields a reasonable plant-based pantry: coconut milk, curry pastes, canned beans, jasmine rice, rice wraps, and rotating dairy-free pasta sauces. No dedicated vegan section, but Southeast Asian and Latin American shelves provide naturally vegan staples a self-catering traveler can build meals around.

stores.name: フェリス食養館 西院店 (Felisu Shokuyokan Saiin-ten)
stores.gf_summary: 食養館 is a specialist natural-food and health-supplement shop (one of 12 フェリス chain locations in Kyoto/Shiga), stocking natural foods, additive-free condiments, nutritional supplements, and organic products — the typical stock for this shop type in Japan includes tamari, rice-flour goods, brown rice, and additive-free seasonings that are useful for GF shoppers. No dedicated GF corner or GF labelling section has been confirmed by sources; shoppers should verify specific items in-store.
stores.vegan_summary: As a natural/health-food specialist with an organic-shop classification, フェリス食養館 very likely carries tofu, natto, and plant-based staples alongside health supplements; macrobiotic-leaning natural food shops of this type in Japan routinely stock soy milk and additive-free plant foods, but specific vegan branded products or plant-based meat have not been confirmed for this location.

stores.name: プレマルシェ・オーガニクス (Premarché Organics)
stores.gf_summary: Premarché Organics is a dedicated natural-food specialist (~3,000 in-store items from a 10,000-item parent catalog) with an explicit グルテンフリー・小麦不使用 (gluten-free/wheat-free) product section and an オルタナティブフード (alternative foods) category. Shoppers can expect to find tamari-style soy sauces, rice-based and konjac noodles, organic GF pasta, and imported free-from brands such as Osawa and Muso alongside allergen-conscious packaged foods.
stores.vegan_summary: Vegan foods (ヴィーガン食品) are explicitly part of the store's range, covering plant-based milk alternatives (including soy milk), soy-based staples (tofu, fermented soy products), and vegan confectionery; the adjoining Premarché Gelateria serves vegan and non-dairy gelato. Imported vegan and organic brands from the parent binchoutan.com catalog (~10,000 SKUs) are stocked selectively in-store.

stores.name: フレスコ 河原町丸太町店 (Fresco Kawaramachi Marutamachi)
stores.chain: スーパーフレスコ (Super Fresco / Heartfriend Co.)
stores.gf_summary: No dedicated gluten-free section or free-from labeling found. As a full-service neighborhood supermarket it will carry naturally GF staples — plain rice, tofu, natto, konjac/konnyaku, atsuage/aburaage, fresh produce, plain meat and fish, eggs — but no identified tamari-vs-shoyu distinction, rice flour (米粉) products, GF pasta, or imported free-from brands. A self-caterer must read labels carefully; no shortcut GF shopping experience.
stores.vegan_summary: Standard Japanese chilled aisle confirmed: tofu, natto, okara, aburaage and atsuage likely stocked alongside konjac products; soy milk (豆乳) is a standard chilled-aisle item at this chain level. Fresh produce section expected; however no plant-based meat, imported vegan brands, or dedicated vegan labeling has been identified.

stores.name: 本と野菜 OyOy (Hon to Yasai OyOy (Book and Vegetable OyOy))
stores.gf_summary: No dedicated GF section or free-from packaged goods. The small retail area sells 坂ノ途中's pesticide-reduced organic vegetables (naturally GF) and ancient/heritage rice (古代米, naturally GF), plus a curated selection of seasonings and dried goods (e.g., a kelp-and-spiced-nut furikake) whose gluten status is unconfirmed. For a self-catering traveler needing tamari, rice-flour products, or labeled GF packaged staples, this is not a useful stop — the café's seasonal vegetable plate is listed as GF-compatible on Vegewel, but that is a dine-in item, not a grocery offering.
stores.vegan_summary: Strong vegan showing on the café side: the signature OyOy季節の野菜プレート is made entirely from plant-based ingredients. Retail purchases skew vegan by nature — organic produce and ancient rice are all plant-based — but the store carries no imported vegan brands, plant-based meat, or soy milk. Self-caterers will find it inspiring but stocked too narrowly to provision a full vegan kitchen.

stores.name: マーケットガーデンKOHYO京都店 (Market Garden KOHYO Kyoto)
stores.gf_summary: As an Aeon-group supermarket, this store carries Aeon/TopValu GF lines confirmed at Aeon stores nationwide: rice-flour bread (Tainai, Nippon Ham brands), rice-based GF udon/yakisoba noodles, TopValu chickpea-and-brown-rice spaghetti, and germinated brown rice pasta. Tamari soy sauce (wheat-free) is a standard condiment aisle staple at Aeon stores. There is no dedicated free-from corner or specialist GF section — shoppers must read labels individually. The 2023 Market Garden relaunch added an organic produce corner (京野菜 + organic veg) but did not introduce a specialty diet section.
stores.vegan_summary: Solid everyday vegan staples: tofu, natto, atsuage, soy milk, fresh organic produce, and konnyaku are standard fixtures. The Aeon group's "Yasashigohan" plant-based frozen line and rice flour gyoza wrappers are stocked at this store format; the in-store prepared foods section (dashi-heavy, fish-based) is largely unsuitable for vegans without checking ingredients.

stores.name: イオンモール京都桂川 (AEON Mall Kyoto Katsuragawa)
stores.gf_summary: The イオンスタイル京都桂川 supermarket (1F, 08:00–23:00) carries AEON's own Top Value GF range: rice-flour noodles (udon-style, Chinese-style, yakisoba, fettuccine), short brown-rice pasta, chickpea-and-brown-rice spaghetti, rice-flour bread, and the "Yasashigohans" frozen line (free from all 8 major allergens including wheat). Also on 1F is オーガニックプラザ (Organic Plaza, opened July 2025, 10:00–21:00), an organic specialty tenant selling rice-flour GF noodles ("おこめん"), traditionally-made GF-friendly condiments, and additive-free organic snacks — making this a notably strong GF-capable stop for a self-catering traveler.
stores.vegan_summary: AEON Style stocks tofu, natto, atsuage, and soy milk chain-wide; the in-mall Organic Plaza (1F) adds direct-from-farm organic vegetables, whole-food condiments, and additive-free snacks well-suited to plant-based diets. AEON's Top Value "Yasashigohans" frozen line (allergen-free pork buns, wieners) and TSURUMEN plant-based tonkotsu ramen round out the vegan-friendly selection.

stores.name: グッドネイチャーステーション (Good Nature Station)
stores.gf_summary: The 1F Good Nature Market is a curated health-food market explicitly stocking gluten-free packaged goods alongside keto, fermented, and organic products — one reviewer describes it as "all keto, vegan/vegetarian, no gluten, fermented, organics, everything." Concrete items include additive-free retort curries, pesticide-free processed foods under the SIZEN TO OZEN brand, and organic rice-based products; a dedicated GF corner is not confirmed, but the overall selection is meaningfully GF-friendly for self-caterers stocking up on snacks, sauces, and packaged goods.
stores.vegan_summary: The market carries organic vegetables, fermented staples (miso, umeboshi), and additive-free packaged foods from health-focused brands; the 4F Hyssop restaurant offers a full vegan menu with botanical drinks. Plant-based packaged options are well-represented given the facility's organic/natural ethos, though dedicated plant-based meat or imported vegan brands are not confirmed on the market floor.

stores.name: プレマルシェ・オーガニクス 京都三条本店 (Premarché Organics Kyoto Sanjo Honten)
stores.chain: プレマルシェ (Prema Co., Ltd.)
stores.gf_summary: Premarché Organics is a specialist natural/organic food shop carrying ~3,000 in-store items from the Prema catalog of 10,000+, which explicitly includes gluten-free products. The parent company's own Prema Shanti line covers gluten-free short pasta (certified by the Celiac Association), gluten-free curry bases, grain-free energy bars, organic rice miso, organic pure-rice vinegar, and tamari-style soy sauces (wheat-free/GF shoyu including quinoa-based options); the store also stocks imported free-from brands (Ohsawa Japan organic tamari, Italian GF pasta). The adjacent Premarché Gelateria uses gluten-free organic mochi waffle cones for allergen-sensitive guests, indicating house-wide GF awareness.
stores.vegan_summary: The store carries a wide range of vegan-suitable products: organic plant-based soups (Mexican beans, Dutch pea, gazpacho, spinach-cashew — all vegan), organic vegetable broths labeled suitable for vegans, plant-based milk alternatives, organic oils (flaxseed, olive), fermented foods (hamanatto, rice miso, rice vinegar), dried fruits, nuts, and organic produce. The company's adjacent gelateria and diner offer dedicated vegan menus, reflecting a consistent house-wide plant-based ethos.

stores.name: Biople by CosmeKitchen 京都 the CUBE店 (Biople by CosmeKitchen Kyoto the CUBE)
stores.gf_summary: Biople's web store carries a dedicated gluten-free category with confirmed GF-labelled items including rice-flour granola (CHAYA macrobiotics 米粉のジンジャー&チアシードグラノーラ) and additive-free GF brownie snacks; the physical food section also stocks organic snacks, superfoods, retort foods, and dried goods where rice-based and GF items appear. There is no dedicated in-store GF corner, so selection varies and shoppers should verify at the shelf — but GF-minded finds are realistic here.
stores.vegan_summary: The store stocks confirmed plant-based items including Minor Figures organic barista oat milk, 100% plant-based dried jujube snacks (VEGE KITCHEN), organic dried fruits, and a curated range of natural beverages and organic pantry staples. Fresh produce and conventional tofu/natto are not stocked — this is a specialty lifestyle shop, not a supermarket — but the curated food section skews heavily plant-based by philosophy.

stores.name: じねんと市場 (Jinento Ichiba)
stores.gf_summary: No dedicated GF section or free-from labelling is evidenced. As a farm-direct produce market the store carries naturally GF staples — fresh Kyoto vegetables, fruit, pesticide-free plain rice (including an unsprayed variety from Kuze, Minami Ward), whole eggs, and local honey/jams. Producer-made miso and pickles are stocked but contain wheat/barley risk typical of Japanese ferments; no tamari, rice-flour products, or imported free-from brands were found in any source. Shoppers should treat processed items as unconfirmed GF.
stores.vegan_summary: The market's strength for plant-based self-caterers is abundant seasonal Kyoto vegetables and fruit sold direct from growers, plus producer-made jams, pickles, and miso (check labels for dashi/bonito). No tofu, natto, soy milk, plant-based meat, or imported vegan lines are mentioned in any source; the adjacent Jinento Shokudo restaurant runs a Kyoto-vegetable buffet that may suit vegans but that is a restaurant, not retail.

stores.name: オーガニック・ベジ・アネックス (Organic Vege Annex)
stores.gf_summary: The store's core offer is pesticide- and chemical-fertilizer-free fresh produce — naturally gluten-free staples like seasonal vegetables, fruits, and root crops. The VEGE DELI prepared-food line (vacuum-sealed curries, kinpira, carrot rapé) uses additive-free seasonings, but no explicit gluten-free labelling, tamari, rice-flour (米粉) products, or imported free-from brands are documented across any source; GF suitability of the deli items should be confirmed in-store before purchase.
stores.vegan_summary: The store is listed on the vegan-focused site Hachidory and the VEGE DELI line is explicitly described as having "many vegan options," including a soy-meat keema curry, seasonal vegetable curry, and tomato pasta sauce — all made with additive-free seasonings and organic vegetables. Fresh produce (the majority of floor space) is fully plant-based by nature.

stores.name: 業務スーパー 三条河原町店 (Gyomu Super Sanjo Kawaramachi)
stores.gf_summary: Gyomu Super chain-wide stocks several useful GF staples: labeled gluten-free rice flour (米粉 1 kg, domestic rice), Thai rice vermicelli (ビーフン), rice noodles made from rice flour and tapioca starch (suitable for pho), rice paper (ライスペーパー), and naturally-GF snacks such as Korean seaweed sheets, jujube chips, and dates bars. No dedicated GF section or imported free-from brands confirmed; tamari availability is unconfirmed at this location — shoppers should check labels in-store.
stores.vegan_summary: Good base for plant-based self-catering: regular tofu and silken tofu (大豆100% domestic), tofu skin sheets (豆腐皮スライス), soy-milk pudding (豆乳花), a soy-protein bolognese sauce, Korean seaweed, rice paper, rice/tapioca noodles, and fresh produce. Natto is typically stocked at this chain. Imported Asian condiments (gapao paste, simple tomato sauces) add variety for vegan cooking.

stores.name: パントリー京都北山店 (Pantry Kyoto Kitayama)
stores.gf_summary: Pantry & Lucky runs a dedicated gluten-free category on its online shop (currently miso-focused) and store reporting confirms vegan and GF-tagged products are stocked in-store alongside organic and additive-free lines. A self-catering GF traveler would find naturally GF staples (rice, produce, plain tofu/atsuage manufactured on-site, miso) and likely tamari-style soy sauce and rice-flour items, but no confirmed standalone GF corner — ask staff or check labels on arrival.
stores.vegan_summary: The store manufactures its own tofu, atsuage, and pickles, and stocks pesticide-free vegetables and organic soy products; the chain's health-food orientation and vegan-tagged product range (confirmed via online shop) make it a useful stop for plant-based self-caterers seeking quality Japanese staples like natto, firm tofu, and soy milk.
